title: Disaster clinic infection control station
summary: Focused extreme medical support for disaster clinic infection control station in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: Even a basic care point needs clean/dirty zones, hand hygiene, sharps control, waste flow, and sick-person separation. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Disaster clinic infection control station”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Disaster clinic infection control station”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Disaster clinic infection control station”.
paper and pen. Context: “Disaster clinic infection control station”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Disaster clinic infection control station»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Disaster clinic infection control station»
steps: Place hand hygiene at entrance and exit.
Separate wound care, respiratory symptoms, stomach illness, and medicine records if space allows.
Keep clean supplies off the floor and away from waste.
Use a sharps container or rigid labeled container for needles and blades.
Assign one person to watch cleaning schedule.
warnings: This is not medical advice and it is not a doctor's advice. It is offline emergency organization guidance only. Use local emergency numbers, trained first aid, pharmacists, clinics, doctors, poison centers, and official public-health instructions whenever available. Do not perform surgery, invasive procedures, diagnosis, prescription changes, or medicine dosing from this guide. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Disaster clinic infection control station”.

Escalate immediately for trouble breathing, heavy bleeding, unconsciousness, severe pain, chest pain, stroke signs, severe burns, pregnancy danger signs, infants in distress, poisoning, or rapid worsening. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Disaster clinic infection control station”.
Do not delay evacuation or professional care to finish a checklist. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Disaster clinic infection control station”.
Keep privacy, consent, dignity, and local law in mind even during collapse. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Disaster clinic infection control station”.
common_mistakes: Trying to diagnose instead of recognizing danger signs while carrying out “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Forgetting times, medicines, allergies, and changes while carrying out “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Touching fluids or wounds without hand hygiene while carrying out “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Moving an injured person without a safety reason while carrying out “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Letting panic, crowds, or rumors control care while carrying out “Disaster clinic infection control station”
alternatives: Use certified first-aid training where available as a fallback for “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Use official emergency services or the nearest clinic as a fallback for “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Use a pharmacist for medicine questions as a fallback for “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Use radio/phone consultation if transport is delayed as a fallback for “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Use written handover notes when devices fail as a fallback for “Disaster clinic infection control station”
failure_signs: Breathing, bleeding, consciousness, pain, fever, confusion, weakness, or skin color gets worse during “Disaster clinic infection control station”
The helper cannot keep the scene clean or safe during “Disaster clinic infection control station”
Transport, water, power, or communication failure blocks care during “Disaster clinic infection control station”
The person becomes unable to drink, speak, walk, recognize people, or stay awake during “Disaster clinic infection control station”
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to replace a doctor, ambulance, clinic, pharmacist, poison center, or trained first-aider when using the “Disaster clinic infection control station” record
Do not use it for surgery or invasive treatment when using the “Disaster clinic infection control station” record
Do not use it to change prescription medicines or doses when using the “Disaster clinic infection control station” record
Do not force care on a conscious adult except immediate life-safety actions allowed by local rules when using the “Disaster clinic infection control station” record
short_term: Reduce immediate danger, call or send for qualified help, keep the person clean, warm or cool as needed, and record facts without guessing. This note applies specifically to Disaster clinic infection control station.
long_term: Build certified first-aid capacity, update medical records, protect supplies, practice handovers, and map routes to pharmacies, clinics, hospitals, and shelters. This note applies specifically to Disaster clinic infection control station.
if_method_fails: Escalate by the fastest safe route: emergency number, clinic, hospital, pharmacist, poison center, radio medical consult, trained first-aider, or evacuation convoy. Record what failed and when. This note applies specifically to Disaster clinic infection control station.
environment_notes: In apartments, shelters, rubble zones, heatwaves, floods, smoke, and outages, medical problems worsen because of crowding, poor air, poor hygiene, no elevator, no refrigeration, and delayed transport. This note applies specifically to Disaster clinic infection control station.

title: Referral queue red-flag sorting
summary: Focused extreme medical support for referral queue red-flag sorting in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: When many people wait for care, a visible red-flag queue prevents quiet high-risk cases from being ignored. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Referral queue red-flag sorting”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Referral queue red-flag sorting”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Referral queue red-flag sorting”.
paper and pen. Context: “Referral queue red-flag sorting”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Referral queue red-flag sorting»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Referral queue red-flag sorting»
steps: Create categories: immediate danger, urgent today, can wait with recheck, information only.
Use observations, not diagnoses.
Recheck waiting people regularly.
Move vulnerable people out of heat/cold/crowds.
Document who was referred, when, where, and by whom.

title: Long-term collapse clinic registry
summary: Focused extreme medical support for long-term collapse clinic registry in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: A community without normal clinics needs a registry for chronic illness, pregnancy, infants, disabilities, medicines, allergies, and follow-up dates. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Long-term collapse clinic registry”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Long-term collapse clinic registry”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Long-term collapse clinic registry”.
paper and pen. Context: “Long-term collapse clinic registry”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Long-term collapse clinic registry»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Long-term collapse clinic registry»
steps: Create opt-in confidential records with minimal necessary details.
List high-risk people and what support keeps them stable.
Track medicine supply, device power needs, mobility needs, and referral routes.
Review weekly or after any disaster event.
Keep backup paper copies protected from theft and weather.

title: Medical volunteer role verification board
summary: Focused extreme medical support for medical volunteer role verification board in offline, delayed-help, or collapse conditions.
content: In collapse, false confidence is dangerous. Roles should match verified training and tasks should have boundaries. This is not medical advice; it is a safety and organization checklist until qualified help is available.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Medical volunteer role verification board”.
soap or sanitizer. Context: “Medical volunteer role verification board”.
gloves or clean barrier. Context: “Medical volunteer role verification board”.
paper and pen. Context: “Medical volunteer role verification board”.
phone, radio, or messenger — for «Medical volunteer role verification board»
labels for time and symptoms — for «Medical volunteer role verification board»
steps: Ask volunteers what training they have and what they are not trained to do.
Assign non-clinical helpers to records, cleaning, transport, supplies, translation, and crowd control.
Do not let unverified people perform invasive tasks.
Pair new helpers with experienced people.
Remove any helper who ignores consent, hygiene, or safety boundaries.
warnings: This is not medical advice and it is not a doctor's advice. It is offline emergency organization guidance only. Use local emergency numbers, trained first aid, pharmacists, clinics, doctors, poison centers, and official public-health instructions whenever available. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Medical volunteer role verification board”.
